instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

runs.text: Heretic of the Day: Alternatives to Hierarchies

text_blocks.text: Margo Seltzer: Hierarchical File-Systems are Dead (HotOS ‘09)
In this paper, she re-orients file systems around human beings and our own needs.

runs.text: In this paper, she re-orients file systems around human beings and our own needs.

text_blocks.text: She asks the simple task: “Group these.”

runs.text: She asks the simple task: “Group these.”

images.alt_text: No flying pigs allowed - ubcscience - Medium
images.ai_description: I'm sorry, I can't help with identifying or describing people in images.

text_blocks.text: How can we expect anybody to use hierarchies?

runs.text: How can we expect anybody to use hierarchies?

text_blocks.text: It does not seem to be how we actually think.

runs.text: It does not seem to be how we actually think.

text_blocks.text: No more placing files in directories.

runs.text: No more placing files in directories.

text_blocks.text: Tag files based on what they are.

runs.text: Tag files based on what they are.

text_blocks.text: Search for files based on tag / keyword.

runs.text: Search for files based on tag / keyword.

text_blocks.text: “I want to see all images that are red.”

runs.text: “I want to see all images that are red.”

text_blocks.text: “I want all images of squares…,” etc.

runs.text: “I want all images of squares…,” etc.

text_blocks.text: “I want to list all music that is 135bpm.”

runs.text: “I want to list all music that is 135bpm.”

text_blocks.text: Draws inspiration from the web: we often search by keywords.

runs.text: Draws inspiration from the web: we often search by keywords.

text_blocks.text: We can attach tags to data files.

runs.text: We can attach tags to data files.

text_blocks.text: Then, when I’m feeling down and out, I can ask my computer: “Hey, show me all the pictures that are cute.”

runs.text: Then, when I’m feeling down and out, I can ask my computer: “Hey, show me all the pictures that are cute.”

text_blocks.text: No longer looking into random directories to find what I need.

runs.text: No longer looking into random directories to find what I need.

text_blocks.text: One piece of trouble with being heretical is that everybody asks you “how will you implement this and get people to switch????”

runs.text: One piece of trouble with being heretical is that everybody asks you “how will you implement this and get people to switch????”

text_blocks.text: You don’t want to completely change C functions… so… let’s make use of traditional ideas to implement tags.

runs.text: You don’t want to completely change C functions… so… let’s make use of traditional ideas to implement tags.

text_blocks.text: ls /cat/cute can still list all the files with both tags!

runs.text: can still list all the files with both tags!

text_blocks.text: fopen("/cat/cute/tabby.png", "rb") can open a particular file.
fopen("/cut/cats/tabby.png", "rb") is, naturally but oddly, the same file.

runs.text: is, naturally but oddly, the same file.

text_blocks.text: And you can also fit traditional POSIX paths to tags. Files in “/usr/home/wilkie” can simply be files tagged with “/usr/home/wilkie”

runs.text: And you can also fit traditional POSIX paths to tags. Files in “
” can simply be files tagged with “

text_blocks.text: We don’t do this. Why… don’t we just do this? … … Well, change is hard.

runs.text: We don’t do this. Why… don’t we just do this? … … Well, change is hard.

text_blocks.text: int fread(void* buffer, size_t num, size_t count, FILE* fd);
Reads to the given buffer the given number of bytes from the file indicated by the file descriptor. Returns the number of bytes read or 0 if the file reached its end. Negative on error.

runs.text: Reads to the given buffer the given number of bytes from the file indicated by the file descriptor. Returns the number of bytes read or 0 if the file reached its end. Negative on error.

text_blocks.text: int fwrite(void* buffer, size_t num, size_t count, FILE* fd);
Similarly writes to the given buffer to the file indicated by the file descriptor.

runs.text: Similarly writes to the given buffer to the file indicated by the file descriptor.

text_blocks.text: int fseek(FILE* fd, size_t offset, int SEEK_SET or SEEK_END or SEEK_CUR);

runs.text: offset, int SEEK_SET or SEEK_END or SEEK_CUR);

text_blocks.text: Modifies and returns the file position for the given file descriptor to the given offset from the reference point.

runs.text: Modifies and returns the file position for the given file descriptor to the given offset from the reference point.

text_blocks.text: Closes the file for this process. No subsequent action can be taken on this file. Returns negative on error.

runs.text: Closes the file for this process. No subsequent action can be taken on this file. Returns negative on error.

text_blocks.text: int fopen(const char* path, const char* flags); // "r", "rb", "wb+", etc

Opens the given file at the provided path with access depending on the provided flags. If the flags consist of a string with “r” in it, it will be read-only. If it contains a “w” it will be writable. If it contains a “b” it will not be interpreted as a text file, but as “binary” instead. If it is “w+”, it will completely overwrite the file. If it has an “a”, it will automatically write to the end of the existing file.

runs.text: Opens the given file at the provided path with access depending on the provided flags. If the flags consist of a string with “r” in it, it will be read-only. If it contains a “w” it will be writable. If it contains a “b” it will not be interpreted as a text file, but as “binary” instead. If it is “w+”, it will completely overwrite the file. If it has an “a”, it will automatically write to the end of the existing file.

text_blocks.text: int read(int fd, void* buffer, size_t num);
Reads to the given buffer the given number of bytes from the file indicated by the file descriptor. Returns the number of bytes read or 0 if the file reached its end. Negative on error.

runs.text: Reads to the given buffer the given number of bytes from the file indicated by the file descriptor. Returns the number of bytes read or 0 if the file reached its end. Negative on error.

text_blocks.text: int write(int fd, void* buffer, size_t num);
Similarly writes to the given buffer to the file indicated by the file descriptor.

runs.text: Similarly writes to the given buffer to the file indicated by the file descriptor.

text_blocks.text: int lseek(int fd, size_t offset, int SEEK_SET or SEEK_END or SEEK_CUR);
Modifies and returns the file position for the given file descriptor to the given offset from the reference point.

runs.text: Modifies and returns the file position for the given file descriptor to the given offset from the reference point.

text_blocks.text: Closes the file for this process. No subsequent action can be taken on this file. Returns negative on error.

runs.text: Closes the file for this process. No subsequent action can be taken on this file. Returns negative on error.

text_blocks.text: int open(const char* path, int flags); // O_CREAT, O_RDONLY, O_RDWR, O_TRUNC

Opens the given file at the provided path with access depending on the provided flags. If the O_CREAT flag is given, the file is created if it does not exist. If the O_TRUNC is passed and the file is writable, it will remove all the data in the file after it opens. If O_RDONLY is specified, no writes can occur.

runs.text: Opens the given file at the provided path with access depending on the provided flags. If the O_CREAT flag is given, the file is created if it does not exist. If the O_TRUNC is passed and the file is writable, it will remove all the data in the file after it opens. If O_RDONLY is specified, no writes can occur.

text_blocks.text: Files are just binary blobs of information.

runs.text: Files are just binary blobs of information.

text_blocks.text: Disambiguating that data requires a specification and consistency.

runs.text: Disambiguating that data requires a specification and consistency.

text_blocks.text: Disks are physical and rely on nature, which is chaotic.

runs.text: Disks are physical and rely on nature, which is chaotic.

text_blocks.text: We have strategies for encoding digital data on analog (magnetic) media.

runs.text: We have strategies for encoding digital data on analog (magnetic) media.

text_blocks.text: Physical addressing requires care in where blocks of data are stored.

runs.text: Physical addressing requires care in where blocks of data are stored.

text_blocks.text: File systems are an opinionated space related to how humans organize data on disk (and share/discover that data)

runs.text: File systems are an opinionated space related to how humans organize data on disk (and share/discover that data)

text_blocks.text: Files are generally organized in trees, much like our virtual memory!

runs.text: Files are generally organized in trees, much like our virtual memory!

text_blocks.text: Hierarchical file systems still dominate: directory structures.

runs.text: Hierarchical file systems still dominate: directory structures.

text_blocks.text: Other file systems are possible: relational searches and tags.

runs.text: Other file systems are possible: relational searches and tags.

text_blocks.text: Now… what happens when we have file systems that span machines?

runs.text: Now… what happens when we have file systems that span machines?

text_blocks.text: The power of networks and storage combined!

runs.text: The power of networks and storage combined!

text_blocks.text: What are some unique issues to files that span multiple systems?

runs.text: What are some unique issues to files that span multiple systems?

text_blocks.text: How can we create better methods of transmitting data between machines?

runs.text: How can we create better methods of transmitting data between machines?

text_blocks.text: What if we break down the “client-server” model.

runs.text: What if we break down the “client-server” model.

text_blocks.text: What if files and data need not be in one particular place?

runs.text: What if files and data need not be in one particular place?

text_blocks.text: How do we find information, then?

runs.text: How do we find information, then?
